Tom Breeze is, like I said earlier, he runs a CPA agency. So a CPA agency is basically a cost per acquisition agency. So instead of an agency model that you're probably accustomed with, it's like 500 bucks to $2,500 a month, what he does is he deals with people who do very high volume, I think is minimum that someone has to be able to take on as like a hundred thousand months for him to take you on as considering a client. And so what he does is he spends his own money and basically says, I'll market your stuff better than you will and you just do all the fulfillment. And so he only uses YouTube, he only uses one platform and he spends about $3 million a month and he spends usually a month-ish, continually testing, changing new videos, changing landing pages, changing sequences, changing video scripts until he finally gets to work, changing audiences, whatever, until we finally get it to work. And so we've been fortunate enough to twist Tom's arm into working with us so that we can do that, so we can use his brain to basically help all of us continue to have new platforms. And so this process that I went through with Tom is what I will be going through with each platform that you guys can imagine so that you have virtually limitless front-end lead generation. All right, I think that's pretty cool. That means, like, we've already got successful campaigns on Snapchat, we'll have that probably released in a little bit. We're right now currently in outreach with a guy who specializes in, he runs a $10 million a year SEO stuff, which I know SEO is dead, only if you're bad at it. He still crushes it. And so what we're going to basically do is be able to spin content for everyone so that everyone has organic leads that are coming in from search, organic leads that are coming in from YouTube, Snapchat, Instagram, Facebook, Google Display Network. And have you guys ever been on the internet and just seen all those ads on the side when you're reading through things? There's ads, that's Google Display Network, that's GDN. That's like the big ocean. And I'm going to be working with the guy who's the guy. I think he's, yeah, same level as Tom, just for Google Display Network and giving him a bunch of money so that he will give us stuff. And I say this because I really want everyone to win. That's all. I just want to be the best and I want you guys to be the best so that you can really conquer your markets. And I have a whole presentation only generation after this. Okay, so that's it. Is Tom, I'm just going to pull Tom up. This is Tom Breeze. Tom Breeze is, I'm not supposed to say this, but he was originally, he was one of the finalists for James Bond. And so Tom, Tom was, I told him a little bit of the story, Tom, but basically how I bought everything on your website and then gave you money and then you said you didn't want the money and then tried to get you to take more money and then finally he took a bunch of money and here we are. So Tom's unbelievable. Like he's really good. Like Tom is an unbelievable marketer and I don't throw that around lightly. He's unbelievable. He's really, really good. I've been able to watch his process in the background and the big thing that he's demystified is that great marketing is working a lot. And so the amount of variations and tests that we ran for the campaigns that he's going to show you was a ton of work. I mean, probably with all the different keyword matches we did, 2,000 campaigns? So very many things that markets are small to finally get it to work. And we reshot the video that you guys have that has like the six-week channel, like the new videos that you guys got in December 23rd. That first one is the one that we've been running on YouTube ads. And so the beauty is that all the competitors that you have who sometimes try and copy stuff still don't do well with it anyways, but the ones you try and copy, you could see our ad and you would have no idea what the placements are and how the targeting works and how the bidding is working. So it's, I don't want to say virtually impossible to copy, it's really hard and you'd need to be like a pro at it. And Tom is a pro. He's really, really good and I'm really excited to have him here. So I know we've already said applause once because you guys are out there, but if you can give him one more round of applause for everything he did for everybody. Thank you so much for that intro, Alex. I found it difficult to hear what you're saying, but I presume it was all good stuff. But yeah, thank you very much for bringing me in here as well. I've actually spent about half an hour before this because I know we're running a bit late, just mucking around with logos. So I thought I'd just show this. So I wanted to start off by saying thank you for that very warm introduction. I also want to say I apologize for not being with you guys. I know Alex was like talking a lot about trying to get me out there and I'd love to have been out there. We've got kind of two very young kids and they're growing real quick and it's difficult to look after them with a small family that we've got. So as a result, it's difficult to move around, especially from being from the UK. So I apologize for not being with you guys, but through the magic of technology, I've had to say thank you again. The reason being is because this is the first time I've given a presentation to around about 200 plus people being naked from the waist down. So it's not great. You actually think I'm joking about that. That's actually not far from the truth. It gets hot in the studio. So anyway, I'm here to talk about YouTube ads today, obviously, and I want to share with you some of the stuff we've been working on that you're going to get to know about how to use this new platform if you're not using it already to start driving leads and new customers and clients to your gyms. And so what I'm going to do is I'm going to walk through it and make sure you just kind of completely get it so that you can start using this yourself as well. The great thing about YouTube and I think a lot of people talk about how many views there are every single day on YouTube and hype it up completely like any platform. I'm not a massive fan of that. I much prefer to kind of just tell you how I think about YouTube as a platform to be advertising on. And really, YouTube is where the world goes to watch video. If you're to go anywhere, obviously, you might see video on other platforms, but this is where you actually go. If you say, oh, I need to watch a video about something immediately in your brain is like, right, I'm going to YouTube. That's where you go for it. There's over one billion users on YouTube. So it's a pretty big space and that kind of counts for a third of the internet. That's just on YouTube. You can even get your videos outside of YouTube as well. So should someone have a video embedded in their blog, for example, you can still run ads to those videos. It's not just YouTube. I mean, you've got the whole internet out there as well that you can be advertising on. So it's a great way of getting in front of potential customers. And just to show you what's happening with YouTube, it's not a dying platform by stretch. It's growing so much every single year and like watch time, the amount of people that are actually on YouTube and consuming content increase 60% in just 2015, which is crazy, I think, to have that sort of growth on a platform that's pretty established already as well. So it's really good on YouTube right now in terms of advertising still hardly anyone's there. So it's a great platform to be on because you'll find that you can get pretty cheap traffic that's very, very high quality. And we'll be talking about that in a few moments. So the thing that I love about YouTube the most out of any platform, because as an agency running traffic for lots of different clients, we just specialize in YouTube. We don't do anything else. We don't go and look at Google tool. We don't look at Facebook at all. I know about it. I've done advertising those spaces before, but I don't concern myself with that at all. Just look at YouTube. But the thing that I love about YouTube the most is that you can target people in the moments that really matter. So because every day, every day people are going to YouTube and looking for information. They will be looking for information. Your market will be going to YouTube and looking for information that you can help them with and convince them to say, right, you're the gym or you're the company that they want to go to and start kind of like getting their solutions from you in that moment. So you're not bombarding people with messages when they weren't necessarily looking for it. You're getting in front of people when they're looking for what you've got. And it's just the perfect moment to get in front of them. And it means you have this really good connection with a viewer if you get your video ad right. And of course, we'll talk about talk through that in a second. So imagine that you've got because I'm just going to focus on the female market for now, because I know that's kind of where our focus has been for the time being as we running these ad campaigns. The if you imagine like people on desktops or people on mobile phones looking out and going to YouTube looking for information, they may start on Google, but they're going to inevitably a lot of them will end up on YouTube at some point. People are going there all the time. And in your market, they'll be typing in things onto into YouTube, things like best weight loss plan, might be how to eat clean, might be hit workouts, for example, or just workouts from home. It could be how to get into shape. People are typing in these keywords all the time. And it means obviously we can use that data if we wish to. But the great thing is, is also Google as the platform it is, is kind of getting this information from everybody all the time. So the artificial intelligence, they're kind of gathering all this information about everybody. And the beauty of that is that they can start kind of attributing certain traits to people. So if you've, let's say, for example, you've typed in how to lose weight in the last 14 days, Google will know that and know that you'll be prime for ads around that sort of content. And it means that as a, as an advertiser, you don't have to just be in that moment. It can, at that moment may have happened an hour ago, a week ago, two weeks ago, and you can still get in front of people, which is just gives you that opportunity to really get in front of people that not just interested, but actually had a, and it had had an intent around that search recently. So it's a great opportunity for us. We can get in front of the people at the time it really matters. Compare that to Facebook, which I know that a lot of you will be very familiar with. Facebook is brilliant, obviously, for loads of different reasons, but it doesn't have this capability. It doesn't have this opportunity to get in front of people based on when they're looking for what you have to offer. So it feels like we're just getting in front of people on Facebook being people that have either shown an inch, like show, like are interested in potentially what we've got from a standpoint of like they just got a general interest in, in kind of being fit or losing weight and that sort of stuff. All they may have been to your website before, or they might be a lookalike audience. The great thing is we've got all of that with YouTube as well. But with YouTube as well, you've also got this search intent, which is really where it just makes it so much more powerful by the very nature of being on YouTube. So obviously with YouTube, it's a kind of like it's a very different platform on sometimes on Facebook, it can feel like it's the newsfeed battleground sort of thing. It's like, how can you get in front of someone's newsfeed in the best possible way, keeping that relevancy score and making sure you get good results there. With YouTube, it's a completely different game altogether. And that's hopefully what I can talk to you about. But as I said, Facebook is about this interest targeting, whereas YouTube is the interest plus the intent targeting as well. We can get in front of people based on the fact that they're looking for what we've got to offer. It makes the whole sales process a lot easier as well. So obviously both platforms do video. And if you've got like a Facebook ad or you've got a YouTube ad that are both video ads, there's pros and cons of both platforms. And it's good because I think that everyone in the audience, well, I don't know if I could be able to see this from my screen. But just by show of hands, how many of you actually advertising on Facebook right now? Okay, so pretty much everyone it looks like. Cool. Well, just to clear that up, is anyone actually not advertising on Facebook? Okay, I can't see any hands going up. That's pretty cool. Okay, cool. And so, yeah, so I think you'll all be very familiar with Facebook. And I want you to know that when you come to advertise on YouTube, it's not very easy to say, you know what, I'm just going to take what we've already got working on Facebook and apply it to YouTube. You almost have to say, right, this is going to be a completely new platform again. And by doing that, you're going to have a much clearer mindset going into advertising on YouTube and get the very best results. So something like view counts. Something like view counts. We have a lot of clients that say to us, oh, we get so many more views on Facebook than we do YouTube. And you will, you will get more view counts on Facebook than you will YouTube. But it's what is a view? So a view on Facebook is sometimes three seconds. I think some people call it 10 seconds as well. I'm not sure how they actually quantify that. It seems to be in a transitional stage right now about how the definition of a view works on Facebook. Compared to YouTube, it's 30 seconds and beyond. So whilst you may have got a lot of people who may have seen the video on YouTube, they won't consider it a view until someone watches past 30 seconds, which means you're getting a really engaged view rather than just someone who's scrolling through their newsfeed. So the video content itself will only trigger a view when someone's really engaged in that content. And talking about engagement as well, you'll probably see that if you look at your if you're running an ad on someone like Facebook, you may see an engagement rate of around about 20 seconds or so for an atypical ad. If you were to copy and paste that video across to YouTube and have that whilst you might get far far less views, your engagement rate might be something like two and a half minutes compared to what you were getting on Facebook. And that's partly because people are going to YouTube to watch video content. Sure. And people are not scrolling through a newsfeed. They're actually clicking to watch a video. But you'll also find that the actual people are paying much more attention to your videos. And this gives you a huge opportunity to what I call stick like Velcro. I have a background in psychology, and that's kind of where I all got started with advertising. And when I when I studied a lot about psychology, prior to the 1980s, a lot of psychologists used to believe that the brain worked in such a way where your memory would be such like a filing cabinet. So it feels like you kind of make it through an experience. And it's very much like what your brain would do is like, right, that goes in that folder, that goes in that drawer, and you close the cabinet. And if you ever want to recall that memory again, it feels like you're kind of pulling open the cabinet and getting that file out again. What they realized was that it's not doesn't work like that at all. It's much more like interconnected kind of memories. So if I was to ask you to think of the first lines of the song, Hey Jude. As soon as I do that, you probably start playing the songs yourself and you probably actually listened to maybe Paul McCartney's voice singing, Hey Jude, and all that sort of stuff, right? My singing voice is not too great, as you can tell. But it is that kind of like you won't just let that memory be like the just remember the lyrics of the song, you'll play the song to yourself. And then you may even have an emotion around that as well. If I was to ask you to think about the taste of a watermelon, it's impossible to do the taste of a watermelon without thinking about the texture of it, without thinking about the green rind and the red fruit in the middle. It's kind of your brain will immediately connect to whatever's going on with that memory. And so it's a lot more interconnected. And that's the beauty you have with video engagement. If someone engages with your video and decides to watch it, they're not just watching it from a standpoint of like, OK, I'm going to sit here and watch. They're consuming so much more information as they're watching that video. And it means that they won't forget you. Now, that means that they might not forget you for good reasons and they might not forget you for bad reasons as well. So you've got to be careful about how you are perceived on YouTube, because if you get it right, they'll remember you and as an awesome company that they want to potentially engage with. And that sticking like Velcro is such a powerful thing, especially when it comes to things like remarketing, which is obviously on Facebook called retargeting. And this is kind of like such a core aspect of any video campaign. We want to make sure we create videos that people connect with. And it sticks in their minds so they don't forget you. Because for you guys in particular, when you've got a local gym, people might watch the video and be like, oh, wow, yeah, I kind of know where those guys are. I think they're just down the road. Oh, yeah, I kind of roughly know where they are. Now, they may not click and decide to actually convert there and then on the ad. Potentially, they may not do that. They might do, but they might not. They might just want to kind of think, oh, I actually know that place. I'll go and drive there and go and kind of see the next week or something and talk to them about this, whatever off they've got going on. And that's the thing is that if you connect with them and they remember you and they remember your ad, they'll know to come and see you. Whereas if it's just like a fleeting visit on a news feed through Facebook, that probably won't happen quite as much. So whilst your online conversions will be very good from YouTube and from Facebook, you may find that your offline conversions also start picking up a lot more just because your video will have that sort of impact and they'll remember it. And it's almost like you become a local celebrity in their mind as well. So all of this kind of sticks together and it starts working really, really effectively for you as a local gym. Cool. So the reason why I would ever go to YouTube as a user, as a consumer of kind of video content, the reasons why you and I will go to YouTube is because we want to either know something. We're looking to learn something, looking to discover more information about something. We want to do something. Potentially we're saying, right, I actually want to kind of like, well, I'm looking up a how to video. How do I do this? How do I do that? You want them to know the actions you need to take in order to kind of achieve something. You go to YouTube a lot because you potentially might want to buy something. I know I go on there when I want to compare models and products because I'll say, oh, is this one versus this one, which one's best? Let's look at a review. I've got two young kids, as I mentioned earlier, and when we first started looking at buggies and strollers for the kids, then I immediately went to YouTube and watched like mums talk about what stroller is best for them. And immediately I was like, great, well, if they think it's the best one, I'm happy to invest in that particular stroller or buggy. And that was kind of like how I would go shopping online for those sorts of products. You won't kind of have that third party to review it for you. So YouTube can be really powerful for that as well. But also sometimes we go to YouTube because we might want to follow someone that we we've watched their stuff before. We're kind of part that we subscribe to their channel. We're looking to find out more information and kind of like get involved more and more. With that business. And so we're going to YouTube because we want to be inspired by leaders and thought leaders that we might like to engage with as well. So these four reasons are probably reasons why you want to write them down, because you'll get to understand why people are using YouTube and how you can connect with them as well. Because if you can't start, if you're able to create these ads that would connect with them, you'll find you have a much stronger idea of exactly what they're looking for as well. OK, cool. So if they're looking, it's a going to YouTube. If people go into YouTube because they want to know something, want to do something, want to buy something, want to be inspired by something, it means they're going to YouTube with a level of motivation already. I know I go to Facebook because I'm pretty much bored and I want to connect with people around me and I might kind of like just say, OK, who's who's kind of like speaking today or who's who's online right now? What are they saying? Who's put a post out, etc. I'm going there purely because I'm a little bit bored, but I might want to connect with people as well. And the people that are using Facebook are typically using it for that sort of reason. There might be others as well. But YouTube is not that sort of place. Like if you're if you're to look at Facebook as like a local bar, for example, people, all the cool guys go or girls go to go to that bar and they hang out and they chat and they talk and it's like that sort of engagement. You're going there because you like I want to be entertained. Whereas with YouTube, people go there almost like it's the library. It's the library of the town, so to speak. You're going there because you want to find information, want to do something, want to learn something, you want to go there because you're a search intent when you're going there. And that means when you advertise, there's going to be very little ad fatigue because you're not bombarding people all the time with the same ad over and over again. They're going to start seeing your ads when they're looking for you and they're going to engage with your ad. And if you get that connection right, then everything works like the algorithm of YouTube and Google AdWords start to love your ads. They start to promote it more often. And then you can start to use some really clever ways of advertising, which we'll talk about that will mean that you can connect to people and not annoy them and bombard them. Because if you're anything like me, most YouTube ads aren't necessarily the sort of thing that I think I can't wait to get up in the morning and watch some more YouTube ads. But it's that type of thing, right? It's kind of if you can get in front of audiences who are looking for what you've got and are ready to kind of receive that content, then you'll find that you get very, very little ad fatigue or so ever. So you won't be having to replace videos each week. You won't need to be able to kind of like have to kind of think about, oh, I've got to create some new creative now or think about some new image ads or text ads or whatever it might be. Once you get a good YouTube ad, you might have to change it once a quarter. Like once every 12 weeks makes your life so much easier. And you can kind of like build these campaigns, optimize them and scale them. And it's kind of a little bit like set and forget. Once you've set it up and it's like kind of working really well for you, like, great, I can kind of walk away from that. And it kind of keeps on getting me 10 leads a day, 15 leads a day, 20 leads a day. It's just working for you. So it's a beautiful platform for that reason. And I love that because it's as an agency, it makes my life a lot, lot easier when we get a campaign working. We'd have to keep on replacing all the creative. But it also becomes consistent and reliable. If people are looking for this, people go into YouTube wanting more information. There's a kind of constant stream of people wanting this all the time. And it means that there's that reliability that people are going to be going to YouTube looking for this information and you're going to be there. So it's this consistency. You're not going to find it ebbs and flows and you have good weeks and bad weeks. You'll find it will kind of become a consistent. Okay, cool. Five, 10, 15, 20 lead a day, just constantly coming through once you've set it up correctly. And also it's high quality traffic because it is search traffic. And they've watched a video of yours, maybe 90 seconds or something. And then clicked your website to find out more. They're not only motivated already before they even saw your ad. They're now seeing your video, watching it and thinking, yeah, that's something that you actually want clicking and come into your website. So they're very, very high quality audiences there as well as they come through. So great for things like retargeting and remarketing as well. But I would say it's not as easy as somewhere like Facebook. When you first look at YouTube, it's quite scary and daunting. So I want to break it down because I think that most of the time people look at YouTube or get into ad words in particular, I think, oh, wow, that looks way too confusing. So a couple of things with this. When you go to YouTube ads and you want to create your video ad, you need to be careful because you need to make sure the quality is high. This is something we learned Alex and I as we were running the ads. We need to make sure that we improve the quality of the production of the ads. That was really important. That was a big breakthrough for us. And we'll talk about that in just a second. But people do expect a level of quality. People do a lot of comparisons of somewhere like TV to YouTube. And it was especially the birth of things like I say birth, but like things like Netflix, Amazon Prime TV, etc. People are getting used to the fact that online there's really good production. And YouTube is just one of those places that it's kind of a video platform. People are expecting good level of video now. So you can't get away with just like a very low kind of resolution video ad. You need to make sure it actually is highly produced and looks the part that will just serve you so much better. There is the dreaded ad words interface, which is scary when you first look at it. But like a big chunk, like probably about 70 to 80 percent of ad words, you can completely ignore when you start advertising with video, because you don't have to learn anything about the search or the image ads or the text ads or display ads or anything like that. You just stick inside your lane of just YouTube and it's just. You know, and campaigns are actually much simpler than people think. But in a way, you want your competition to keep on believing that YouTube is really complicated because they'll never touch it then. And you've got a kind of free open field just to advertise without your competition there, which will make your life a lot easier. But any new venture, any new advertising, eventually get into. Obviously, it can feel like sometimes there's a lot of potential wasted ad spend, which is the beauty of obviously what Alex and I have been doing is that we've been kind of breaking it down, working it out for you. So we can kind of give you the campaigns and say, is what's working or here's what seems to be working the best right now. Because there is very little how to information out there. If you go and look up how like YouTube ads training, I know even from my perspective, I've done a couple of trainings before in the past. But the stuff you'll be learning here is stuff I never teach because there's a good reason for that. And we'll talk about that in just a minute. It's true, though, that there is a need to diversify. You've probably seen that with places like Facebook, Zuckerberg recently was talking about the fact that they're not going to show so many ads in the news feed to hopefully give a better user experience for people. You might have seen that with Facebook as well, it's like they're starting to explore a lot more opportunities, which might not necessarily work that well. You've kind of things like the WhatsApp ads now I see quite a bit of, a messenger, et cetera, as well. I know Instagram, I'm not loving as much as I used to, because I see a lot more advertising now. So it's just like advertising is inevitable. It will come to any platform, but it needs to be right. And then as soon as a company starts saying, well, we can't start giving more inventory out, we can't kind of like keep on expanding this ad inventory for our customers, then you know that what's going to happen is going to get more competitive and the prices are going to start going up. If you were advertising on Facebook four or five years ago, you would have had like the time of your life and now it's getting, it's still great, but it's good time to start diversifying now as well, to start looking at other platforms that you can use to start advertising, to get your customers as well. And this is where Alex came to me. So obviously, as soon as I saw his name come through, I had heard of Alex before as well, because I've done some work with ClickFunnels and things. And when Alex came through, I was like, yeah, I know that name. And so I did a Google search immediately. I was like, ah, okay, this guy looks cool. Looks very handsome and kind of pretty well built out and things. And then I saw that he actually looks like this when I jump on a call with him. I was like, ah, okay, cool. Very different. And whilst I was looking for these photos as well, Alex, I found these as well. That's you and your much younger. And even this one as well, which I thought was there. I love that. Anyway, let's move on. So Alex, now I obviously were chatting and talking about things. And he was very keen for me to work with him, but I was the kind of like, when we first came on board, I was like, look, do you know what? I've got to be careful about who I work with because an agency, we're a Premier Google partner and we're just normally focused on like the biggest campaigns we can think of, like international campaigns where we can spend a lot of money and make a lot of money, of course, as well. We've got a lot of clients that we work with that are kind of pretty well known. A lot of clients that aren't that well known, like more corporate type brands, et cetera, as well. And I know that Alex has been talking about kind of like monthly ad spend and things, but we are always looking to build like those international campaigns. It's kind of it's the big markets for us that we can really scale and really kind of run those campaigns at a much bigger level. And when when we're running these international campaigns, there's a certain way of doing it. I've got very good at running these campaigns to get the very best out of them. We spend a lot of money every single day a month on advertising on YouTube as well. And we also work on CPA deals. So instead of being a monthly ad spend with clients, like a percentage that we would share with them, or instead of being a monthly fee, what we do with clients instead of say, hey, look, I'll fund the advertising. You pay me based on results. And so we have a much better relationship with clients and it means we can both scale together. I mean, I say that is because when I spend my money, you know, the stuff that I do and I'm sharing with you is stuff that actually works. Otherwise, I wouldn't have a business. So this is it's a kind of great place to be. But I remember like Alex, when we first have a chat, I was like, Alex, I'm not sure if we're necessarily the best fit for each other because I'm normally running international campaigns like Tom, Tom, Tom, just just like just hear me out like Alex, I'm really not sure. And then he was like, look, Tom, just just just work with me on this one. You're going to love working with me. And I was like, Alex, I'm really not too sure. And he says, I'll pay you this much. And I was like, sure, OK, cool, let's get going. So yes, Alex and I kind of like started working with each other as a result of kind of really trying to crack a new market, really. And my typical action plan that I'll go with most clients is to say, right, first of all, let's deep dive and do our research as to kind of exactly where we're going to get the customers from. And on a local campaign, it's kind of very, very different. So immediately I was like, OK, research is going to be very different to what we used to. We can't get into the kind of trends that we tend to see across nations. It's working very differently on a local campaign. And typically I'll go into heavy production with things like video scripting. Once we know that research, we'll do like a really elegant shoot as well. And then I've got an edit team here as well. So we kind of like create the perfect video ad for any market. Then we'll build the best campaigns we know. And again, that's kind of like for those international campaigns. Then we optimize things really rapidly. And then we scale like what we find. That's kind of like the process we go through. And it kind of rinse and repeat. Keep on building, optimizing and scaling and building, optimizing and scaling. And we started working, nothing worked. So I kind of built out these campaigns. I was like, Alex, this is going to be so cool. You're going to love me. And then like we turned on the ads and I was like, OK, Alex, we got to have a chat. Things weren't working as well as we thought they would. And so we've got a bit more of like back to the drawing board. And that's kind of where I just kind of thought, right, well, this whole local campaign idea is going to be very different to what I'm used to. And it was perfect for me because I love challenges. I love kind of working out how to make it work. But I knew that Alex was in it for the long run with me as well. So it was kind of a case of like we had run like even in the first kind of couple of weeks, we had already built out loads of campaigns and kind of like start to kind of like see different things we were doing, loads and loads of ad groups inside those campaigns. And a lot of it was just like just not active. Like nothing was hitting, nothing was working. I was like, I didn't know what was going on and couldn't work at all out. So I had like local learnings. I was like, as I went along, the first one that I had was the targeting has to be very different to get in front of your customer. So search can be too restricting to get the traction very big from the very beginning. So the campaigns that we built when we first got started, I was looking to kind of like really hone in on people's search behavior, what they're looking for. So we can kind of say, OK, if they look for that, we'll be there every single time, no matter what. And what ended up happening is we kind of go for things like keywords, which is like if they've typed in a keyword recently, and that's kind of the last hour or so, we'll get in front of them. If the placements was very much like if they're watching a video related to what we think would be right for us. Let's say, for example, it was a weight loss video or a weight loss journey video or kind of how to get into shape or how to do a home workout and all this sort of stuff. We're thinking, right, there's many placements, like different videos out there that we can advertise in front of. Nothing happened. And then I was like, right, well, let's go for some topic related. Targeting as well, which is like they'll group certain videos together based on their topics. I said, if they watch those videos, those topics, we'll see what happens. And again, nothing really was happening. We've got kind of like a couple of views every single day, but it was nothing that was going to ever set the world on fire for this sort of campaign. So nothing really was just getting any stats in. And it was just a bit of a confusing time for me, because I was like, this is our stop, go to campaigns, we start going global. And so we realized that we kind of like a few of the targeting options were out from the beginning. So we had to go a lot broader. I mean, now we're starting to get back into the keywords and the topics and the placements, and we're starting to get much better traction now. But it's because we've got that momentum and we kind of once YouTube gets to know you, it allows you to expand a little bit more as you go. So I started looking a lot more with Alex at audiences and demos. And we looked at demos only. So we said, right, let's just go look for demographics only. And it's like females. And then we're going to break down all the different age categories. So on YouTube, it's like 18 to 25 or 18 to 24. Sorry, then it's 25 to 34, 35 to 44, 45 to 54. And then 55 to 64 and then 65 plus. So it breaks down into these categories as you're running your ads. And we start to get traction. So it was just literally like everybody in in the area that we were advertising and anyone who was female between the ages of like, I think we just said not 18 to 24. So I think I can't remember off to my head now. But if we said, right, anyone who's female will start seeing these ads and we'll just kind of like run these ads should they go on YouTube and look at videos. So it was like hardly any targeting apart from just females on their mobile devices and also on the desktop devices as well. And of course, we start to get traction. We start getting views that were from the right place. But of course, when you're advertising a local product to say, right, we've got a gym just around the corner from you, it's a lot more of a pull. If I'm kind of like advertising a product or a service that's international and there's not that there's never a kind of a local connection there. It obviously means it's a bit more difficult to make that instant connection. But if you are advertising what we were advertising in the Australia region and you're kind of like start off the ad and there's like graphics on the screen saying, hey, we're targeting Australia ladies. Then immediately it's like, oh, wow, that's that's me. It kind of calls them out immediately. It starts the video ad. And so that had obviously a lot better connection from the video. But cost per lead was never going to be able to be dropped. It was still a little bit too high. So it wasn't the perfect solution from the get go. The cost per lead was still a little bit too high. So we had to kind of see what we could do to reduce it down a little bit. And that was when we kind of discovered local learning number two, which was lowering the cost per lead, how to do that in the most efficient way. Now, on average, our cost per view, which is someone watching in past 30 seconds, was around about two cents. And of course, we could have done our work to potentially made that one sense. But as soon as you go to one sense on YouTube, you're kind of getting beaten by everybody, everybody everywhere. So you kind of like you're never going to get that much traction when you go down to one sense per view. You I don't want to go down to that level because we just would be dealing with much more difficult data to attain. So I didn't really go much lower on our cost per view. Process. But I needed to get more value or more connection from the video. I need we need to get more juice out of the campaigns by improving the video ad as opposed to kind of reducing the targeting any further. So what we did is we took the existing video, which is basically just taken straight from Facebook and we're running on YouTube. And we scripted a whole new video ad. And later got down to the studio and what kind of like Alex and the team produces amazing video like real high quality, send it to us. We had our edit team put like some B roll together for it as well. And just the quality just jumped up a level. And that was a big breakthrough for us because increasing that productivity sort of production quality, improving that, meant that people would see it and immediately give it more credibility from the outset because people are expecting that better production quality on YouTube. So by just changing the ad, this was an ad that had been tested and tried and tested. I'm sure many of you have used it before in the past as well from Facebook. It's like Alex knew this was like a really, really strong ad. And I was like, I did a lot of convincing to say, look, I really recommend a different ad and we went and did it. And immediately we made it a lot more YouTube friendly. So it's like tapping into what people are going to YouTube for in the first place. And we found that really works well. Now, the reason why it works well is not just that, but also remember that when you've got a if you're trying to comparisons between Facebook and YouTube on Facebook, remember, you might use a video ad to grab a load of connect like attention from people. But then people tend to read what's around the video ad. So they might see the video, I might stop them, their tracks are going through their news feed and they're like, oh, what's that? And then they'll read what's going on. And they might say, read more. And there's a whole big kind of that bit next to like it talks them through exactly what the offer is. And when you have that, you'll find that people kind of will have a lot more engagement with your ad on Facebook by actually doing the reading around that video. Whereas on YouTube, you don't have that. You just literally have the video by itself, especially if you're running it as a in-stream ad, which is like the pre-roll ads on YouTube. And we recommend that process to run those in-stream ads because you just get a lot more exposure to kind of forcing yourself into their viewership, so to speak, because you can pay to be in front of other videos that are about to watch. So with YouTube, you don't have this text around the video. So the video itself has to carry the whole message, has to do all the heavy lifting of getting someone to turn from a viewer into a lead for you. And so that itself was a big learning for. I knew it was true for myself, but I was like, I've taken high converting video ads from Facebook before. And even when they're high converting, I still find we can improve it by just going back to the basics of how to create a really, really good video ad. And just to give you an idea of what we would do to script a really, really good video ad, because of production quality, obviously you can hire a video team and get them come in. They can create something really, really powerful. The script is really important. And we use a formula that you might want to write down called Aducate. And Aducate is actually an acronym, so we'll go through each one of the letters here. And the first part of your script, you're going to want to make sure you focus on the aim of your viewer. What are they looking for? What they actually kind of want to when they go to YouTube? Are they looking for to kind of lose weight? So what's the biggest goal they're looking to achieve at that moment in time? At that moment when they're on YouTube looking for something, what is it they're looking for? And we have to start there with the video ad, help them realize the video. They're just about to watch this video ad that's really well produced is going to answer exactly what they're looking for. If they get down the first five seconds and they realize that, wow, yeah, this kind of seems like this video ad is exactly what I'm looking for. It connects with what my aim is to find this information. You'll find you'll grab their attention immediately. So make sure you start with a viewer's aim in mind. The second thing you want to do is make sure you bring in the difficulty that they're experiencing. What is it? Why they can't currently achieve their biggest goal? What are they looking to achieve? Why they are new to you looking for information about how to help them achieve it? What's standing in their way from getting that goal? Now, as as informed gym owners, you may actually you actually know the answer better than they will. The viewer might not even know why they're not achieving the goal. I think they probably could. They might have their own perceptions of what their difficulty is. And you might know that the actual answer is different to what they're thinking. What you need to do is make sure that you connect with whatever the viewer's difficulty might be, like whatever their perception of the difficulty might be. So if I, being someone who doesn't necessarily know too much about physical fitness and or have these sorts of things of like, I want to lose weight or anything like that, I'm more kind of like I keep healthy and all sorts of stuff. I don't know. I'm not in the in I'm not in the know. In that scenario, I think there's certain difficulties that are having my brain. And if you don't connect with what my difficulties are, even though you might be right, if you don't connect with what I think is right, you're actually going to lose me as a viewer. So even though you could tell me what, like how I'm thinking incorrectly, you need to still connect with what I'm thinking. It's quite a difficult thing to get your head around sometimes, especially when you're like, got the not like the curse of knowledge, so to speak. So the reason I say the curse of knowledge is because I know as a YouTube ads expert, when I'm when I kind of like talking to clients, I know at an age you see level what the typical things are that comes up for them as a like their pain points is why they're not achieving the goals they want to achieve. And I know they're kind of somewhat like it's different to what I would think, so to speak. And it's because sometimes I'm a little bit too close to it because I know it's so well. And it's I had a really good analogy. It feels like sometimes you're you're sitting in the jar and you can't read the label, so to speak. It can sometimes feel like that for a lot of people. Like when they're too close to what they actually do and what they they know, it can feel sometimes they can't get in the in the brains of their customers. And it's much easier for a third party to do that. So sometimes it's a good idea to kind of like talk with people or talk with people about like what is it that's stopping them from achieving their goal and then kind of trying to use that as part of this difficulty. It's a bit of a it's a bit of a funny one, but it's when you get it, it can be a really powerful part of that video ad. Then you need to make sure once you've kind of explained that difficulty and it connects with them. You need to make sure that they know that you understand and you know you understand the emotion that comes with. And so if you say, hey, look, I know it feels like it can feel like this sometimes. And if you can really connect with that same emotion, they'll be like, oh, yeah, that's exactly how I feel. As soon as you that you'll be kind of well on your way to really connecting with them as a viewer. Then you want to build your credibility. Now it's not going to be difficult to do if you're a local gym owner because you're going to have a gym and therefore it's going to be kind of like, right, tick in the box. Immediately they feel like, yeah, you're the expert. You know what you're talking about. You're positioning yourself as an expert and someone who can help them. You're not trying to be the expert and say, look, look how great I am as a as a as a as a company or as a gym or anything that you might be saying, like as an expert. It's not for your position to say you're the expert. It's more a case of like you can really help them as a guide for the viewer to become the hero of the story. So now the viewer to feel like, wow, I've I've now kind of can I can now break through this difficulty and achieve the goal I actually want and I become the hero. That's kind of how you need to make sure that you position yourself. So it's not necessarily saying, here's how great I am. A couple of sentences just so they can trust you is fine. That's all they need. They just need to be like, I can trust you to be my guide to make me the hero. That's kind of where credibility comes in. Then you just make sure you can tell that there is an action plan. Now I know that you guys there's a three step process that you guys use with. The nutrition plan, the workouts as well and the making sure you're keeping them on their game as well. And using that as the action plan work really well there. But then you want to go to the next level of that as well and break some of that down so they can really understand it by teaching them something. So let's say, hey, look, this is why this works incredibly well. Let me just walk you through a couple of these bits that will kind of like just show you how this is different to everything else you might have done before and why it works so well. And then just break down a couple of bits there. And at the end of the video, then it's obviously the call to action, the exit part of the video. You're going to say, hey, look, click the link in the video and come to the website, sign up and we'll really look forward to working with you very, very soon indeed. And obviously kind of position that kind of like offer exactly as you need to. Now, if you were to go through this educate process and make notes to begin with and then write it out as a script and then just like play around with it and get comfortable with it. You can write. And that's something that I've just used time and again with any client is the is the go to scripting process that I'll go to whenever creating any script for any client and it's worked so well for us. So I recommend you use that formula. So having used that and we created that video later, we found that the cost per lead massively dropped. That's when we started getting leads in for around about 10 to 15 dollars. So we knew we're in the kind of like we're moving the right direction. And as soon as I see some traction, as soon as I see the cost per lead start dropping, I'm like, great, I can, I can sort this now. I know, I know we'll get there. I just need to tweak a few more things, optimize a few more things to get it perfect. But also the other thing as well, Alex was reporting back to me and saying, look, we're getting higher conversions from the lead. You're generating for us as well. So whilst like the cost per lead might be 10 to 15 dollars or so much be higher than what you're normally used to from someone like Facebook. What we are finding is those people are much more likely to convert, which is really encouraging. I was really excited by that because it's like the value of each lead was therefore worth more. So that's kind of what you tend to find from YouTube traffic to someone like Facebook traffic because it is search and it is video and people are going there with that intent on YouTube. You tend to find the cost of acquisition of a lead might be a little bit more than someone like Facebook, but you might find they make up for it for the fact that they actually convert more as well. So it can kind of be a bit of swings and roundabouts with that process. And that brings me to the local learning number three from here as well, is that there were certain nuances that I had to play with to see if it's just an algorithmic thing. And what we're finding is that like even like location and radius targeting when it was tweaked, it was like, wow, this is working so much better. So what we started off with was a seven mile radius around the gym. And it was kind of working okay. I was finding it was like not getting as much exposure as I thought it could. And I was a bit kind of like concerned about why that might be. So we said, right, well, let's just play around with it and let's see what we can do. And one of the first things we did was say, well, let's broaden it. We did actually did to 10, 15 and 20 miles. Actually, no, I don't think we did 20. I think we did 10, 12 and 15 or something. I just broadened it out. But it was interesting because seven miles didn't work that well. 12 miles didn't work that well. 15 miles would do that well. 10 miles seemed to be some sort of algorithmic kind of sweet spot. And immediately just got so much more traffic that also converted really, really well. So it was it was really interesting because even when you get a conversion still within that seven miles, it was just getting a lot more traction, getting a lot more exposure, kind of got that momentum going. And 10 miles seems to be the sweet spot. So the location targeting, whilst you can choose a radius, it's sometimes going to be really interesting from a standpoint of testing out and thinking, well, maybe let's not go 10, maybe go 11, 12, maybe go eight, nine, whatever it might be, just to play around with it and see which one tends to get that sweet spot because 10 miles for this location has been proved to be so much better, not just for conversion rates, but also for the amount of scale that you can get as well. So I feel like there might be some sort of algorithm thing there that's just really, really worked out well for us here. And once we start getting these cost-believes down, we're looking to kind of increase this scale as well. So having tested all these different miles and kind of seeing that 10 miles is best, that kind of really gave us this opportunity to start seeing a lot more data and optimizing a lot quicker as well. Now, having kind of gone through those learnings and kind of seen what's working to increase in the quality of the video, changing the location, targeting, some of the broadening out with some of the targeting as well, I was then thinking, what can we do to start honing this in? And we're still doing this process now. We've kind of nailed in pretty well already, but I know there's a lot more work I can do on this. And so we have been and we continue to work on this right now. We're starting to zero in on the winners, which is the obvious thing to do. So as soon as you start seeing some really good results, you're like, right, let's really, really focus in now and see if we can get better results from the existing campaigns. And we're seeing really, really good results by segmenting the audiences out. So there's so many different targeting options you get when you're working with YouTube. So somewhere like Facebook, people obviously got your interest targeting, you got your lookalikes. And also you've got your kind of like your interest, your lookalikes and your retargeting as well. With YouTube, it's broken down to so many different types of targeting that's available to you. And it's really just because you're borrowing Google's data and that Google have had this data for many, many more years. And so they're kind of like they've just honed in on different types of targeting. Two of these targeting types, which are in market audiences and affinity audiences, are starting to look really exciting for us because they're not only can not see much reduction in scale, but they're also improving the cost per lead dramatically. So an in market audience is when you define an audience based off if someone's search behavior lends itself to a category. And so there'll be these different categories you can choose. And in the very near future, I'm talking about like four to five months away from here, you can actually define and build your own in market audiences, which is going to be really exciting. But let's say, for example, when you look at your in market audiences inside of Google, what you'll tend to see is that there'll be a huge array of these different in market audiences. Even things like people who are going on holiday to Hawaii in the next couple of weeks. And the reason why they'll know that they have all this data is because Google are tracking all the information and they see your renting cars, you're buying flights, you're buying hotel rooms and all sort of stuff. They'll they'll just know that that adds up to you're probably going to be going on a holiday and they'll know your destination as well by the fact that you booked it through a certain airline. And it kind of like hits to kind of like, oh, wherever you're kind of searching for car rentals, for example, they'll know what's where you're going. But that's just the holiday market. You can it's got kind of everything. If you're looking for baby related products, it's got in market audiences to say in the last seven to 14 days, you've been searching for that type of content. Therefore, you fall into this in market audience. And when we're advertising to all the demographics like around like so just like females of a certain age group, you're going so broad in a market. And when I started applying in market audiences, what I actually did is I turned on all the market audiences because we might as well because we're just saying, right, well, still, let's just advertise to absolutely everybody in this this 10 mile radius now. But just by defining the difference in market audiences, they would fall into then you can start turning off the different in market audiences that aren't working for you, leaving you with the market audiences that are working incredibly well for you. And the cost per lead at this point is down to like six, six dollars, seven dollars per lead coming through and at this rate. And we still got a bit of work to do on it to scale it up in those little in market audiences. But it's exactly the same with the affinity audiences as well. So we're starting to kind of like see the affinity audiences, which is where you would have a hobby around something. So you're continuously continuously kind of going to certain websites and searching for information. So unlike an in market audiences where you're recently been searching for something and affinity audiences where you are always looking for that sort of stuff. You can you can segment again by affinity audiences and you can start to see exactly what people are going for or what they're looking for. And you can advertise to them based on that and start honing in something even like shopaholics, for example, which is an affinity audience tend to convert incredibly well. And I don't necessarily look at the label too much from what AdWords would call it. I'm just more interested in the fact that it converts so carry on going, so to speak. And it just it's a really great process because it's it's difficult and it's hard work from our end to work this out. But the great thing is once we worked it out, Alex will be able to just say to you, OK, here's the five affinity audiences. Here's the 12 in market audiences go and that will work really, really well for you. So we're in a great place by finding these out now for you guys. As I say, the cost per lead is coming down to six to twelve dollars now reliably and I'm getting really excited by that. There are a couple of things we're going to be working on as well because the website that we're promoting at the moment, I believe could be improved dramatically. It works really well from Facebook traffic, but I feel like we can do a lot better job on the website. Alex noticed this early on because he was saying like, wow, like Facebook is converting around about twenty five to thirty five ish percent when we're kind of sending traffic from Facebook. But on YouTube, it's like down to like 15 percent. We're not getting the conversions come through. And I believe it's a quality thing. So it's in a really high quality video ad and they're getting to this page which would convert really well from Facebook. But because people are still expecting that higher quality and this is my belief, it's not necessarily tested just yet. We're looking to say, well, let's design a better page that we can have that's just like beautiful. So when they come from the ad, it's kind of like that seamless branding experience. So the ad scent is called the ad to the landing page is very, very similar experience. And that really helps conversion rates. So we're going to be looking at designing a slightly better website that would obviously hope to bump up those numbers. Because if we can bump up those numbers of conversion rates from YouTube to, let's say, twenty five to thirty five percent would be in a great place. So that's going to be a brilliant place because not only will the cost believe drop, but it means we can kind of go scaling out. A lot more in different areas. So it all kind of has knock on effects here. Also, we're going to test it out with a landing page video. So we're going to test out having a part one experience on YouTube. And it's like part two would be on the landing page of the website as well. So I'm going to be trying out that process soon. So have a video landing page as opposed to just a text landing page. And also, we've been recently talking about the idea of doing a quiz landing page. I know I work with a lot of people in the fitness space from selling global products. And we dramatically improved the conversion rates of some of their products by actually having a quiz for that engagement level at the very beginning. So we'd say instead of like, hey, go and buy this product about how to get into the best shape of your life. And instead, what we said is like, hey, look, before you try and get in the best shape of your life, fill in our quiz because we'll be able to ask you a few kind of like different questions about your your gender, your age, what sort of body type you are right now. And we'll be able to prescribe the perfect workout regime. It's going to work really, really well for you. So kind of we'd go through this process. And at the end of it, we could be a lot more tailored with our responses so we could segment these audiences out. And we're looking to build something very similar in this process as well. Obviously, then Alex can build out with you guys as well to copy and paste it. But we're testing that idea of that shortly to see if we can get some really, really good results from that. And what are we working on next? So what we're going to be doing shortly, I know that Alex and Laila are going to be going down to the studio and creating some more ads. We're going to build it out. So it's like what's what we call cascading videos. Now, the beauty of this is that it's working with things like remarketing in this process. So we know that if we keep on using the same ad in the same location, whilst we don't necessarily get affected by ad fatigue per se, we feel like we could give a better user experience and that might improve the amount of scaling we can do as well. And the idea being is that if we start off with video ad one and we get it out to a large audience, we can then say, right, well, video ad one has gone out. Now let's exclude those people who have seen video ad one from seeing it again. So as a user's experience, like someone in a local market, kind of like going on to YouTube, they might see your ad once, they never see it again. And they get followed up with video ad two that kind of continues the story. And you're like, OK, of course, it's not the same ad, but I've seen I've seen these guys before and that was a different type of kind of angle in that video. Maybe the first one was about workouts. The second one was about nutrition, for example. And then we stop them from seeing video ad two ever again. And then we show them video ad three. So it kind of cascades through. It's like a sequence of videos that people go through. So your user experience will be very much like you see one ad, then you see the next one, then you see the next one. And if I don't convert after three ads, we kind of put them on what we call a naughty list so they don't see any more ads, which means it can keep it nice and cheap for us as well. Because we're not going to be constantly spending money on the wrong people. So we do this and we've done this in a lot of markets. It's worked really, really well. We're not sure if it's going to work in a local market, but that's what we're going to test it. But it means it also can kind of like build a story around your gym as well and around the whole process and the whole kind of like idea of what is your promoting. So it feels like someone's not just getting a gym membership, they're getting the right kind of advice on workouts, nutrition, getting people kind of like making sure they're kind of keeping on track, etc. And all of this so it kind of like the customer gets to learn a lot more from you and get to love you as well because that whole process becomes a lot more personable. And it's all automated and it's all designed to kind of help you scale and get better ROI. The perfect thing is it's also focusing on the user experience, which means that when people turn up, they're like, do you want to see your video ad on YouTube? And I absolutely loved it. And I saw like two or three, actually, they're all kind of like just really, really cool. That's hopefully the relationship we can get as we start creating more and more of these ads in the local markets. Plus also what we found is that when we were advertising, we found that like the 45 to 55 market and the 25 to 34, actually it should be 34 there. Those two age groups converted the best. We're working really well. 35 to 44, not as good. I'm not sure why, but like 25 to 35 and then 45 to 55, you guys probably know better than I do because you guys get to work with the customers and things. But these groups tend to convert really, really well. And I was like, well, if someone's 25 versus someone who's 54, for example, completely different aims and desires and difficulties stand in their way. So what we're going to do is we're now not just going with one ad, we're going to go with two different types of ads. One for someone who would be 45 to 55 and one that would be 25 to 35 and have a different message, a different ad for these different audiences and wish I'm sure that's going to have a much better connection with this audience as well. So we're working on stuff all the time and the beauty is that I know that we're working with a whole mindset of making sure that we can give this stuff to you guys as well. So your huge advantage is coming very soon. We're at a place now where we can kind of like, I think we're a week or two away from being able to say this is the way to do it. And I know that Alex is going to be kind of like breaking it out for you and just making sure it's super simple to follow. But it's almost like all the hard work's been done already. So it's a really useful place to just give you that and allow you to see the engagement. But it's been to the end and thank you so much. Does everyone appreciate how big this is? Okay. So the goal is that we can, if we can diversify platforms from a lead generation standpoint, then we can virtually scale. Like we can make sure that every gym is at full capacity always and that creates scarcity, which then drives up prices, which drives up margins. Right. So if I can basically guarantee that you always have a full gym, we can double your margins, the same gym, because there's a waiting list. Because you have so many leads come in the front door. You see how big this is? Okay. It's big. All right. So I think it's worth it because of how much Tom worked on all of our behalf. Can we just give him one more before I welcome him to come on stage? All right.
